plainMeaning: Here the reciter bows, one by one, to the first eight of the twenty-four great Jain teachers — from Ṛṣabha and Ajita through to Supārśva and Candraprabha.
recitation: usabha majiaṁ ca vande, saṁbhava mabhiṇaṁdaṇaṁ ca sumaiṁ ca | paümappahaṁ supāsaṁ, jiṇaṁ ca caṁdappahaṁ vande ||

wordByWord.gloss: Ṛṣabha — the first Tīrthaṅkara (object of vande)
wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit usabha < Sanskrit ṛṣabha, 'bull' (Ṛṣabha's iconographic emblem).
wordByWord.notes: Stem form standing as accusative object of the following vande.

wordByWord.etymology: ajiaṁ < Sanskrit ajita, 'unconquered'; the leading m is a euphonic āgama (hiatus-filling consonant) inserted between usabha and ajiaṁ.
wordByWord.notes: The word is ajiaṁ (Ajita); the m belongs to no name — it merely bridges the two adjacent vowels. Accusative singular.

wordByWord.etymology: First person singular present of √vand, 'to praise, salute, bow'.
wordByWord.notes: First of two occurrences; brackets the list of names (see second vande at the close).

wordByWord.etymology: abhiṇaṁdaṇaṁ < Sanskrit abhinandana, 'rejoicing, delight'; again preceded by the euphonic āgama m.
wordByWord.notes: As with m-ajiaṁ, the m bridges saṁbhava and abhiṇaṁdaṇaṁ; the name is Abhinandana. Note Prakrit n → ṇ. Accusative singular.

wordByWord.etymology: sumaiṁ < Sanskrit sumati, 'good mind, sound wisdom' (su 'good' + mati 'mind').
wordByWord.notes: Accusative singular; intervocalic t softened/lost in Prakrit (sumati → sumai).
Third ca of the verse, closing the first hemistich's tally.

wordByWord.etymology: paüma (< Sanskrit padma, 'lotus') + ppaha (< prabhā, 'radiance') = 'lotus-radiant'; his emblem is the red lotus.
su ('good, fair') + pāsa (< Sanskrit pārśva, 'side, flank') = 'fair-sided'.
Prakrit jiṇa < Sanskrit jina, 'conqueror (of the passions)'.
wordByWord.notes: An epithet here syntactically governing Candraprabha, but applicable to all named — a reminder that each is a Jina.

wordByWord.etymology: caṁda (< Sanskrit candra, 'moon') + ppaha (< prabhā, 'radiance') = 'moon-radiant'; his emblem is the moon.
wordByWord.notes: Accusative singular; pairs imagistically with paümappahaṁ — two 'radiance' (ppaha) names within one verse.
Second vande, closing the inclusio opened by the first; the eight names are framed between two acts of bowing.

literalTranslation: Ṛṣabha and Ajita I venerate; Sambhava and Abhinandana and Sumati; Padmaprabha, Supārśva, and the Jina Candraprabha I venerate.
idiomaticTranslation: I bow to Ṛṣabha and to Ajita; to Sambhava, to Abhinandana, and to Sumati; to Padmaprabha and Supārśva; and to the Jina Candraprabha I bow.

elaboration.verseByVerseCommentary: Having vowed in the previous verse to praise all twenty-four, the reciter now begins the actual roll-call, naming Tīrthaṅkaras one through eight in their exact canonical order: Ṛṣabha, Ajita, Sambhava, Abhinandana, Sumati, Padmaprabha, Supārśva, Candraprabha. The verse's craft lies in two features a careful reciter should notice. First, it is bracketed by vande — 'I bow' opens the verse and 'I bow' closes it — so the eight names are held within an embrace of veneration; the single verb does devotional duty for every name. Second, twice an extra m appears at a word-junction (usabha **m**-ajiaṁ, saṁbhava **m**-abhiṇaṁdaṇaṁ). This is not part of any name but a euphonic consonant (āgama) that Ardhamāgadhī inserts to smooth the meeting of two vowels — a point on which newcomers (and even some glosses) stumble, mistaking 'majiaṁ' for a separate figure. The name is simply Ajita. Note too that several of these are 'speaking names': Paüma-ppaha is 'lotus-radiant', Caṁda-ppaha 'moon-radiant', Sumai 'sound-minded', Supāsa 'fair-sided' — so the very act of naming already praises each one's distinguishing light or virtue. The lone epithet jiṇaṁ ('the Jina'), grammatically attached to Candraprabha, in spirit crowns all eight: each named teacher is a conqueror of the inner foes.
elaboration.doctrinalContext: The verse enacts nāma-smaraṇa / kīrtana — devotion through precise remembrance of names. In Jain thought a Tīrthaṅkara's name is guṇa-niṣpanna, born of his qualities, so to utter 'Padmaprabha' or 'Candraprabha' is already to recall the radiance such a being embodies. Bowing to all eight with one undifferentiated vande affirms the equality of the perfected: among Jinas there is no rank, only the same conquered, liberated state. The naming is not mythography but a spiritual census of those who have actually crossed, offered as living proof that the path can be walked to its end.
elaboration.practicalRelevance: When chanting, the reciter should let each name land as a distinct bow rather than blur the list into sound — pausing inwardly on each of the eight. Practically, mark the euphonic m so as not to fracture or invent names (Ajita, not 'majiaṁ'; Abhinandana, not 'mabhinandana'). Because Logassa also times kāyotsarga, accurate, unhurried articulation of this naming-verse keeps the breath-count honest.

crossReferences.text: Caturviṁśati-stava (Logassa), gāthā 2 — the naming of Tīrthaṅkaras 1–8 within the Second Āvaśyaka
Standard sequence of the twenty-four Tīrthaṅkaras, Ṛṣabha through Mahāvīra

sources.title: Tirthankara
sources.consultedFor: Canonical order and identity of the twenty-four Tīrthaṅkaras, confirming names 1–8 in this verse.

sources.title: Padmaprabha
sources.consultedFor: Identity of the sixth Tīrthaṅkara and the lotus emblem/red complexion underlying the name paüma-ppaha ('lotus-radiant').

sources.title: Chandraprabha
sources.consultedFor: Identity of the eighth Tīrthaṅkara as 'Lord of Moon' and the moon emblem behind caṁda-ppaha ('moon-radiant').

sources.title: Suparshvanatha
sources.consultedFor: Identity of the seventh Tīrthaṅkara (Supārśva, 'fair-sided') confirming the gloss of supāsaṁ.

notes: The names follow the standard canonical order 1–8 (Ṛṣabha, Ajita, Sambhava, Abhinandana, Sumati, Padmaprabha, Supārśva, Candraprabha), matching the preface's stated arc. The leading m in 'majiaṁ' and 'mabhiṇaṁdaṇaṁ' is a euphonic hiatus-filling consonant (āgama m), not part of any name; some online glosses err here, variously misreading 'majiaṁ' as Padmaprabha or as Sanskrit madhyam — both are incorrect, the word is Ajita. Verbatim orthography of the Anchalgachchha (Vidhipakṣa) source is preserved (e.g. ṇ for n, ppa conjuncts, anusvāras); no normalisation applied. Tokenisation here keeps the euphonic m with its following name for clarity; it could equally be analysed as a junction-consonant standing apart.
